main_summary.summary: Oliver W. Hill Jr., son of NAACP civil rights attorney Oliver W. Hill Sr., was interviewed in Richmond, Virginia on August 17, 2013 by David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project. Born in 1949, Hill Jr. grew up in segregated Richmond, where his father practiced with Spottswood W. Robinson III on cases tied to the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund (LDF). He recounts the household environment of telephone threats and a cross burning, and traces his father's path from Roanoke to Dunbar High School in Washington and Howard Law School under Charles Hamilton Houston and Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, where his father was a classmate of Thurgood Marshall. Hill Jr. describes the Farmville student walkout led by Barbara Rose Johns and the resulting Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County case, the strategic pivot from separate-but-equal to attacking segregation per se, and the Byrd Machine's massive resistance under Governor J. Lindsay Almond Jr. He was one of the first Black students in Richmond to desegregate the schools, entering Chandler Middle School in junior high. He attended Howard University in the late 1960s, studied Eastern philosophy and meditation, became a meditation teacher in Boston and Detroit, then earned a psychology graduate degree at Michigan and built his career at Virginia State University, later collaborating with Bob Moses and the Algebra Project in Petersburg.
key_themes: NAACP Legal Defense Fund litigation strategy
Howard Law School and Charles Hamilton Houston's training
Barbara Rose Johns and the Farmville student walkout
Massive resistance and Richmond school desegregation
Black education and the Algebra Project
main_summary.historical_significance:
Hill Jr. offers first-person testimony on the domestic and legal infrastructure behind the Brown v. Board cases from inside the Hill household: the rotating residence of NAACP lawyers who could not stay in segregated Richmond hotels, the breakfast-table strategy sessions involving Thurgood Marshall and Robert L. Carter, and his father's incremental campaign to overturn Plessy v. Ferguson. He provides direct recollection of Barbara Rose Johns's call to his father, the strategic shift from separate-but-equal to attacking segregation per se, and the family connection between Barbara Rose Johns and her uncle Vernon Johns.

The interview also documents one Black child's experience of being placed in the desegregating Richmond schools roughly seven years after Brown, the social geometry of Chandler Middle School in the early 1960s, the later Judge Robert R. Merhige Jr. busing decision that included surrounding counties, and the William Rehnquist non-recusal that produced the 4-4 Supreme Court split overturning the cross-county remedy. Hill Jr.'s post-movement trajectory through Howard during the black power era, meditation teaching, psychology research at Virginia State University, and partnership with Bob Moses on the Algebra Project links the civil rights legal generation to contemporary education reform in Petersburg.

chapters.title: Richmond Childhood and Family
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. introduces his 1949 birth in segregated Richmond, the self-contained Black community structure around Virginia Union University, and the threats and cross burning his family faced because of his father's NAACP work. He describes how his parents shielded him from racism while preparing him for the role of desegregating Richmond schools.

chapters.title: Father's Path to Howard Law
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. traces his father Oliver W. Hill Sr.'s upbringing in Roanoke and Washington, attendance at Dunbar High School, and entry into Howard Law School in the late 1920s under President Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and Dean Charles Hamilton Houston. He describes Houston's social-engineer charge and Hill Sr.'s classmate relationship with Thurgood Marshall.
related_events: Howard Law School transformation under Houston

chapters.title: NAACP-LDF and Virginia Cases
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. describes his father's work with the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund founded in 1940 by Thurgood Marshall, the Virginia teacher salary case of the 1940s, his father's World War II service, and Hill Sr.'s 1948 election to the Richmond City Council as the first Black elected official in the Deep South since Reconstruction.
related_events: Morgan v. Virginia interstate bus segregation case

chapters.title: Barbara Rose Johns and Davis Case
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. recounts Barbara Rose Johns's call to his father from Farmville, the student walkout at the Black high school with tar paper shacks for overflow, and the lawyers' strategic shift from separate-but-equal to attacking segregation per se. He notes Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County became part of the Brown package, and identifies Vernon Johns as Barbara Rose Johns's uncle.
related_events: Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County

chapters.title: Massive Resistance in Virginia
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. describes Virginia's massive resistance led by the Byrd Machine and Governor J. Lindsay Almond Jr., the pupil placement board mechanism that controlled school assignments, and his father's working relationship with school board head Lewis F. Powell Jr. He distinguishes between desegregation and integration as his father saw the difference.

chapters.title: Desegregating Chandler Middle School
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. describes entering Chandler Middle School in seventh grade as one of the first Black students to desegregate Richmond schools, the school board measuring distances by yardstick to keep Black students out, the surreal first day with a partial walkout, and the gradual social integration through football teammates over years.

chapters.title: Busing, White Flight, and Annexation
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. describes Judge Robert R. Merhige Jr.'s busing order covering Richmond and surrounding counties, the threats and killed dog Merhige endured, William Rehnquist's non-recusal producing a 4-4 Supreme Court split that overturned the cross-county remedy, the Richmond annexation moratorium under the Voting Rights Act, and the morph of race politics into class politics.

chapters.title: Howard University and Inward Turn
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. describes attending Howard University in the late 1960s during the black power era with Stokely Carmichael and H. Rap Brown on campus, a Howard course in African history that opened him to civilizations he had never heard of, and his turn toward Eastern philosophy, meditation, and a perception course that drew him into psychology.
related_events: Howard University black power era late 1960s

chapters.title: Loss of Black Teachers
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. reflects on the high quality of teachers in segregated Black schools, the unintended consequence of integration that drained the Black teacher pool as the women's movement opened other careers, and the spiraling decline of schools in Petersburg where he now works with four superintendents turning over since 2005.

chapters.title: Recognition of His Father's Legacy
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. describes the Arthur Ashe statue controversy, the objection to a Lincoln statue at the Tredegar Iron Works, the Virginia Civil Rights Memorial on the Capitol grounds depicting Barbara Rose Johns alongside his father and Spottswood W. Robinson III, and the building on Capitol grounds named for his father where Harry F. Byrd Sr. had once strategized massive resistance. He notes Clinton's 1999 Presidential Medal of Freedom to his father.

chapters.title: Psychology Career and the Algebra Project
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. describes his career at Virginia State University researching Black education and cognitive factors, the malleability of cognitive skills challenging 1950s heredity claims, and his partnership with Bob Moses and the Algebra Project in Petersburg. He explains Moses's approach to abstract math teaching grounded in children's life experiences and his civil-rights model of community-based school reform.

chapters.title: Education as Civil Rights Battleground
chapters.summary: Hill Jr. critiques Virginia's Standards of Learning curriculum-narrowing, characterizes contemporary urban schooling as a national disgrace, and frames education and mass incarceration as the continuing civil rights battleground. He references Michelle Alexander's The New Jim Crow and connects the prison-industrial complex to collapsed family structures and inner-city schools.
related_events: Virginia Standards of Learning testing regime
